tool_input.summary:
1. Clarifying Questions — None.

2. Interpreted Scope — I interpret “redshifted olivine exposures” as Martian surface olivine-bearing units identified spectrally (e.g., via CRISM VNIR/IR absorption-band positions) and your question as a request for PDS datasets you can use to (a) map those exposures and (b) compare their thermal behavior (e.g., temperature/emissivity/thermal inertia proxies) by location/area. Assumption: target is Mars; primary olivine-mapping instrument is MRO/CRISM; thermal context can be obtained from Mars thermal-IR instruments (TES/Mini-TES; THEMIS not located in the catalog results available in this run).

3. Search Plan —
- Catalog-first discovery in PDS_CATALOG (pds_catalog_search_tool) for CRISM archives (PDS4 bundles + PDS3 volumes) and for Mars thermal-IR/emissivity datasets.
- Cross-version check: include both PDS4 and legacy PDS3 identifiers when they appear to represent the same CRISM product families.
- Where a dedicated Mars thermal inertia dataset was not surfaced by the catalog queries, return the closest thermal-IR surface products found (TES surface emissivity recalibration products; Mini-TES derived emissivity) and note the gap.

These are the datasets that directly match your query based on the stated constraints (Mars; olivine mapping + thermal comparison), and here are additional datasets that can also help answer the question.

4. Curated Candidate Dataset Shortlist (ranked; PDS4 first)
- CRISM map-projected reflectance (for areal/locational mapping of olivine spectral parameters)
- CRISM VNIR hyperspectral RDR (for band position/“redshift” characterization)
- CRISM multispectral RDR (for broader coverage/context)
- CRISM DDR geometry products (for precise geolocation/photometric geometry when comparing areas)
- TES-derived surface emissivity (thermal-IR surface property proxy to compare among exposures)

5. Additional Candidate Datasets
- Legacy PDS3 CRISM targeted RDR / DDR volumes (often cited in literature and still used)
- CRISM type spectra library (reference spectra for olivine/band-shift comparisons)
- CRISM atmospheric retrieval/correction ancillary products (for consistent spectral analysis)
- Rover Mini-TES derived emissivity (in situ thermal-IR emissivity at MER sites)

6. Candidate Dataset Metadata (what each provides / where to get it)
- MRO CRISM MTRDR (PDS4 bundle): map-projected RDRs with geospatial referencing; use to delineate exposure polygons/areas and extract location.
  Download/landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-mptargeted-v1/mrocr_4001/
- MRO CRISM VRDR (PDS4 bundle): VNIR hyperspectral RDRs; use to compute/inspect olivine band centers and quantify “redshift” consistently across exposures.
  Download/landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-vnirhyperspectral-v1/mrocr_7001/
- MRO CRISM MRDR (PDS4 bundle): multispectral RDRs; use for wider-area context and to extend mapping beyond targeted scenes.
  Download/landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-multispectral-v1/mrocr_3201/
- MRO CRISM DDR (PDS4 bundle example): derived geometry/ancillary needed for accurate map projection and spatial/thermal cross-comparison.
  Download/landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mro/mro-m-crism-6-ddr-v1/mrocr_1014/
- MGS TES surface emissivity (PDS4 derived collection): thermal-IR surface emissivity products (useful thermal property context to compare among exposure locations/areas).
  Download/landing: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/PDS4/mgs_tes_recalib_atmos/data_derived_surf-emissivity/

Additional:
- CRISM Targeted RDR (PDS3 volume): legacy targeted RDR archive.
  Landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/missions/mro/crism.htm
- CRISM DDR (PDS3 volume): legacy derived data records (geometry) archive.
  Landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/missions/mro/crism.htm
- CRISM Type Spectra (PDS3 volume): reference/type spectra library often used for mineral ID comparisons.
  Landing: http://crismtypespectra.rsl.wustl.edu/
- MRO-CRISM atmospheric retrievals (PDS4 bundle/collection): atmospheric retrieval products that can help make spectral comparisons more consistent across locations.
  Landing: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/PDS4/mro-crism_atmos-db/
- MER Mini-TES derived emissivity (PDS4 bundles): in situ emissivity products (site-specific thermal-IR surface property context).
  MER1: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mer/mer1-m-mtes-4-emr-v1/mer1mt_3xxx/
  MER2: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mer/mer2-m-mtes-4-emr-v1/mer2mt_3xxx/

Missing/uncertain in this run: a dedicated Mars Odyssey THEMIS thermal inertia/global thermal products dataset did not surface via the catalog queries available here; if you specifically need THEMIS-derived thermal inertia or daytime/nighttime IR brightness temperature mosaics at orbital scale, that would require a targeted THEMIS-specific catalog/registry lookup beyond the matches returned in this run.

7. Decision Gate (what to do next)
- If you share the specific exposure regions (e.g., named features or bounding boxes) and what “thermal behavior” metric you want (thermal inertia vs brightness temperature vs emissivity), the next bounded step is to:
  (a) pull CRISM products over those areas (MTRDR/VRDR + DDR),
  (b) pull TES/THEMIS thermal products over the same footprints, and
  (c) compare by co-registered location/area using the lat/lon metadata in the product labels.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM MTRDR Bundle
candidates.reasoning: Provides CRISM map-projected targeted RDR products suitable for mapping the areal extent and location of olivine-bearing (including ‘redshifted’ band-center) exposures. Likely related to legacy CRISM map-projected/targeted RDR PDS3 volumes (relationship not fully confirmed here). Download/landing page in catalog: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-mptargeted-v1/mrocr_4001/.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM VRDR Bundle
candidates.reasoning: Contains VNIR hyperspectral CRISM RDR products needed to evaluate olivine spectral band positions (i.e., quantify ‘redshift’) consistently across different exposures/locations. Likely related to legacy CRISM RDR products in PDS3 (not proven 1:1 here). Catalog landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-vnirhyperspectral-v1/mrocr_7001/.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM MRDR Bundle
candidates.reasoning: Multispectral CRISM RDR products provide broader spatial context for olivine-bearing units and help compare exposures across larger regions before focusing on hyperspectral targeted scenes. Catalog landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-multispectral-v1/mrocr_3201/.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM DDR Bundle - 1014
candidates.reasoning: Derived Data Records (DDR) provide geometry/ancillary information needed for accurate geolocation and consistent comparison across areas (e.g., when pairing spectral exposures with thermal datasets). This is one DDR bundle instance; other DDR bundle partitions exist. Catalog landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mro/mro-m-crism-6-ddr-v1/mrocr_1014/.

candidates.title: MGS TES Atmospheric Recalibration Derived Atmospheric Opacity Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Provides TES-derived surface emissivity products (thermal-IR surface property context) that can be sampled at the same locations/areas as CRISM olivine exposures to evaluate whether different exposures show similar or different thermal-IR behavior. Catalog landing: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/PDS4/mgs_tes_recalib_atmos/data_derived_surf-emissivity/.

candidates.title: MRO-M-CRISM-3-RDR-TARGETED-V1.0
candidates.reasoning: Legacy PDS3 CRISM targeted RDR archive used in many published mineral-mapping studies; useful for cross-checking against PDS4 bundles and for older workflows. Catalog landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/missions/mro/crism.htm. Likely related to PDS4 CRISM TRDR/MTRDR/VRDR families (exact mapping not confirmed here).

candidates.title: MRO-M-CRISM-6-DDR-V1.0
candidates.reasoning: Legacy PDS3 CRISM DDR archive (geometry/ancillary) supporting geolocation and cross-dataset comparisons by area. Catalog landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/missions/mro/crism.htm. Likely related to PDS4 CRISM DDR bundle partitions (not confirmed 1:1 here).

candidates.title: MRO-M-CRISM-4-TYPESPEC-V1.0
candidates.reasoning: CRISM type spectra library can provide reference spectra for olivine and related materials, supporting consistent interpretation of ‘redshifted’ band positions across multiple exposures. Catalog landing: http://crismtypespectra.rsl.wustl.edu/.

candidates.title: Atmospheric Retrievals for Mars Integrated from MRO-CRISM Derived Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Atmospheric retrieval products tied to CRISM observations can help normalize spectral comparisons across locations (important when comparing band positions and linking to thermal behavior by area). Catalog landing: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/PDS4/mro-crism_atmos-db/data_derived/.

candidates.title: MER1 Mini-TES Derived Emissivity Data Bundle
candidates.reasoning: In situ Mini-TES derived emissivity at MER Spirit site provides ground-truth-style thermal-IR surface properties (emissivity) for comparison, though only at rover traverse locations (not global exposures). Catalog landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mer/mer1-m-mtes-4-emr-v1/mer1mt_3xxx/.

parts.content:
ROLE
You are the Planetary Data Discovery Agent (NASA PDS Dataset/Product Finder).
Your job is discovery and metadata only: translate a user's planetary-science question into bounded searches across NASA PDS discovery tools and node-operated services, then return relevant bundles/collections/datasets/products with stable identifiers and download locations when available. Do not download anything.

OBJECTIVE
Given a user query, you must:
1. Interpret the request without inventing facts.
2. Ask for clarification only when the query is too ambiguous or too broad to search responsibly.
3. Choose the right search granularity and tool type for the request.
4. Return the strongest matching result(s) with required metadata, and include both PDS4 and PDS3 versions when available for the same underlying data or product family.

SCOPE
Inputs may include:
- a natural-language planetary science query
- optional constraints such as target, region, mission, instrument, time, resolution, geometry, processing level
- optional prior run output for Stable vs Latest comparison

In-scope data sources (PDS-only):
PDS node websites and node-operated services (GEO/ATM/IMG/PPI/RMS/SBN).

Node/Service families and typical tools:
- GEO → ODE_MCP
- IMG → IMG_MCP
- RMS → OPUS_MCP
- SBN → SBN_MCP
- PPI → PDS4_MCP / PDS_CATALOG_MCP
- ATM → PDS4_MCP / PDS_CATALOG_MCP
- Catch-all / breadth → PDS_CATALOG_MCP
- Catch-all / breadth → PDS4_MCP

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- No downloads, carts, email flows, or password-protected workflows
- No scientific interpretation or conclusions
- No non-PDS result sources
- No invented identifiers, hierarchy, or metadata
- No subjective endorsement language such as "best," "top," or "most suitable"
- If the user asks for bulk scraping or unbounded retrieval, ask them to narrow the request
- Refuse requests involving credentials, access-control bypass, or restricted access

SEARCH RULES
1. Do not invent facts.
   You may apply minimal retrieval-oriented normalization, such as expanding common mission or instrument aliases or standardizing target names. If you do, state it explicitly.

2. Search at the correct granularity.
   - First decide whether the request is primarily about:
     - bundles, volumes, collections, or datasets
     - specific observations, granules, or products
   - Granularity determines what kind of entity to return, but not the initial routing step.

3. Use catalog-first routing for both dataset-level and product-level searches.
   - If the user is looking for bundles, volumes, collections, datasets, observations, granules, or products, first search with broad catalog-style discovery tools:
     - PDS_CATALOG_MCP
     - PDS4_MCP
   - Use these tools first to identify the best matching candidate datasets, collections, bundles, product groups, or product families.
   - During broad catalog-first discovery, explicitly check for both PDS4 and PDS3 representations when available, rather than stopping after the first matching version.
   - After identifying strong candidates, narrow with node-specific tools only when needed to:
     - refine results
     - retrieve more specific product-level matches
     - confirm node-specific metadata
     - obtain stable product pages, endpoints, or download locations

4. Use node-specific tools as a narrowing or follow-up step.
   - After catalog-first discovery, narrow using the mapped node/service when appropriate:
     - GEO → ODE_MCP
     - IMG → IMG_MCP
     - RMS → OPUS_MCP
     - SBN → SBN_MCP
     - PPI / ATM → usually remain in PDS4_MCP or PDS_CATALOG_MCP unless a node-specific follow-up is clearly needed
   - Do not begin with node-specific tools unless catalog-first discovery is impossible or the user explicitly requires a known node/service workflow.

5. Broad-first is the default for all discovery-style queries, including dataset-level and product-level requests.
   - Start with PDS_CATALOG_MCP and/or PDS4_MCP.
   - Then narrow with filters or node-specific tools as needed.
   - If a search returns no useful results, relax constraints rather than stacking more filters.

6. Exact identifiers are a special case.
   - If the user provides an exact dataset ID, LID, LIDVID, PRODUCT_ID, OPUS_ID, or ODE_ID, you may go directly to the most appropriate resolving tool.
   - Even in this case, use only the minimal additional calls needed to confirm metadata, parent context, or stable access paths.
   - If relevant, still check whether a corresponding PDS4 or PDS3 counterpart exists.

7. Version preference and cross-version coverage.
   - When relevant data exists in both PDS4 and PDS3 forms, return both.
   - Prefer PDS4 first in ranking and presentation, but also include the corresponding PDS3 version if available.
   - Do not stop after finding only one version.
   - Clearly label each result as PDS4 or PDS3.
   - Describe cross-version relationships only when supported by identifiers, titles, descriptions, archive lineage, or node metadata.
   - If the relationship is uncertain, mark it as likely_related or unknown rather than assuming equivalence.
   - When a matching PDS3 result is found, also check whether a corresponding PDS4 version, migration, successor collection, or equivalent product family is available.
   - When a matching PDS4 result is found, also check whether a corresponding legacy PDS3 version exists when it is still relevant for discovery or comparison.

8. Stop when you have a strong answer.
   - If a dataset, collection, or product clearly matches the user's query, stop broad exploration.
   - Make only the minimal extra calls needed to complete required metadata, parent context, or one representative lower-level example if relevant.
   - Do not keep searching just to pad the number of results.

9. Avoid search loops.
   - If repeated searches with the same tool are not improving results, switch tool type or return best partial results.
   - Do not re-fetch an entity already confirmed unless needed to fill required metadata.

10. Allow partial success.
    - If some facets succeed and others fail, return the successful results and clearly label unresolved parts.
    - Use a hard stop only if the whole request cannot be searched responsibly.

DEFAULT WORKFLOW
Interpret → Clarify only if needed → Choose granularity → Search broad first with PDS_CATALOG_MCP / PDS4_MCP → Check for both PDS4 and PDS3 representations when available → Narrow with node-specific tools if needed → Execute bounded searches → Collect candidates → Dedupe → Attach one parent level up when available → Return results

OUTPUT FORMAT
Use Template A by default.
Use Template D only when the request cannot be searched responsibly.

Template A — Primary Structured Output
1. Clarifying Questions — only if required; otherwise "None."
2. Interpreted Scope — target/region/mission/instrument/phenomenon/constraints/normalizations
3. Search Plan — routing rationale, tools to query in order, fallback behavior
4. Curated Candidate Dataset Shortlist — 1–5 results, ranked by semantic match, PDS4 first when paired with PDS3
5. Additional Candidate Datasets — up to 5 alternates if genuinely useful
6. Candidate Dataset Metadata — for every returned candidate, include source_service, node, entity_level, identifiers (logical_identifier/urn for PDS4; DATA_SET_ID/PRODUCT_ID for PDS3), version_info (data_standard, related_version_identifiers, version_relationship), title, description, parent (one level up), download (direct_url or stable archive path/endpoint), why_this_matches, missing_metadata
7. Decision Gate — what to expand/narrow/compare next

Required framing language for Template A:
- "These are the datasets that directly match your query based on the stated constraints..."
- "...and here are additional datasets that can also help answer the question."

Template D — Hard Stop
1. Hard Stop Trigger — what cannot be determined; 1–3 clarifying questions with why each matters
2. Next action for the user

FINAL BEHAVIOR
- Be precise, neutral, and metadata-focused
- Do not claim execution unless execution occurred
- Do not invent missing fields
- Prefer bounded results over unsupported completeness

OUTPUT-SCHEMA BRIDGE
Your final_result must conform to the FindDatasetOutput / PDSCatalogFindDatasetOutput schema attached by the runtime:
- Place the curated shortlist and any additional candidates in the `candidates` array.
- For each candidate, set `dataset_id` to the PDS3 DATA_SET_ID or PDS4 logical_identifier, `title`, `mission`, `node`, `pds_version` ("PDS4"/"PDS3"), and `reasoning` (the why_this_matches text plus any version-relationship note).
- When a PDS3<->PDS4 pair exists, emit one candidate per identifier (do not collapse them).
- Place the Template A narrative (Interpreted Scope, Search Plan, framing language, decision gate) in the `summary` field as plain text.

BENCHMARKING MODE — IMPORTANT
This run is an automated benchmark against a fixed query set. You will receive
ONE query and must produce ONE final_result. There is no human in the loop and
no follow-up turn.
- Do NOT ask clarifying questions. Always use Template A.
- Do NOT emit Template D. If the query is ambiguous, pick the most plausible
  interpretation, note the assumption in `summary` under "Interpreted Scope",
  and proceed.
- Never wait for confirmation. 
- Hard cap: at most 12 tool calls per query. If you reach the cap without a
  strong match, emit your best partial candidates and stop.
- Empty candidates is acceptable only if every reasonable search has been tried
  AND returned nothing; explain that in `summary`.

parts.args:
{"summary":"1. Clarifying Questions — None.

2. Interpreted Scope — I interpret “redshifted olivine exposures” as Martian surface olivine-bearing units identified spectrally (e.g., via CRISM VNIR/IR absorption-band positions) and your question as a request for PDS datasets you can use to (a) map those exposures and (b) compare their thermal behavior (e.g., temperature/emissivity/thermal inertia proxies) by location/area. Assumption: target is Mars; primary olivine-mapping instrument is MRO/CRISM; thermal context can be obtained from Mars thermal-IR instruments (TES/Mini-TES; THEMIS not located in the catalog results available in this run).

3. Search Plan —
- Catalog-first discovery in PDS_CATALOG (pds_catalog_search_tool) for CRISM archives (PDS4 bundles + PDS3 volumes) and for Mars thermal-IR/emissivity datasets.
- Cross-version check: include both PDS4 and legacy PDS3 identifiers when they appear to represent the same CRISM product families.
- Where a dedicated Mars thermal inertia dataset was not surfaced by the catalog queries, return the closest thermal-IR surface products found (TES surface emissivity recalibration products; Mini-TES derived emissivity) and note the gap.

These are the datasets that directly match your query based on the stated constraints (Mars; olivine mapping + thermal comparison), and here are additional datasets that can also help answer the question.

4. Curated Candidate Dataset Shortlist (ranked; PDS4 first)
- CRISM map-projected reflectance (for areal/locational mapping of olivine spectral parameters)
- CRISM VNIR hyperspectral RDR (for band position/“redshift” characterization)
- CRISM multispectral RDR (for broader coverage/context)
- CRISM DDR geometry products (for precise geolocation/photometric geometry when comparing areas)
- TES-derived surface emissivity (thermal-IR surface property proxy to compare among exposures)

5. Additional Candidate Datasets
- Legacy PDS3 CRISM targeted RDR / DDR volumes (often cited in literature and still used)
- CRISM type spectra library (reference spectra for olivine/band-shift comparisons)
- CRISM atmospheric retrieval/correction ancillary products (for consistent spectral analysis)
- Rover Mini-TES derived emissivity (in situ thermal-IR emissivity at MER sites)

6. Candidate Dataset Metadata (what each provides / where to get it)
- MRO CRISM MTRDR (PDS4 bundle): map-projected RDRs with geospatial referencing; use to delineate exposure polygons/areas and extract location.
  Download/landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-mptargeted-v1/mrocr_4001/
- MRO CRISM VRDR (PDS4 bundle): VNIR hyperspectral RDRs; use to compute/inspect olivine band centers and quantify “redshift” consistently across exposures.
  Download/landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-vnirhyperspectral-v1/mrocr_7001/
- MRO CRISM MRDR (PDS4 bundle): multispectral RDRs; use for wider-area context and to extend mapping beyond targeted scenes.
  Download/landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-multispectral-v1/mrocr_3201/
- MRO CRISM DDR (PDS4 bundle example): derived geometry/ancillary needed for accurate map projection and spatial/thermal cross-comparison.
  Download/landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mro/mro-m-crism-6-ddr-v1/mrocr_1014/
- MGS TES surface emissivity (PDS4 derived collection): thermal-IR surface emissivity products (useful thermal property context to compare among exposure locations/areas).
  Download/landing: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/PDS4/mgs_tes_recalib_atmos/data_derived_surf-emissivity/

Additional:
- CRISM Targeted RDR (PDS3 volume): legacy targeted RDR archive.
  Landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/missions/mro/crism.htm
- CRISM DDR (PDS3 volume): legacy derived data records (geometry) archive.
  Landing: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/missions/mro/crism.htm
- CRISM Type Spectra (PDS3 volume): reference/type spectra library often used for mineral ID comparisons.
  Landing: http://crismtypespectra.rsl.wustl.edu/
- MRO-CRISM atmospheric retrievals (PDS4 bundle/collection): atmospheric retrieval products that can help make spectral comparisons more consistent across locations.
  Landing: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/PDS4/mro-crism_atmos-db/
- MER Mini-TES derived emissivity (PDS4 bundles): in situ emissivity products (site-specific thermal-IR surface property context).
  MER1: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mer/mer1-m-mtes-4-emr-v1/mer1mt_3xxx/
  MER2: https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/mer/mer2-m-mtes-4-emr-v1/mer2mt_3xxx/

Missing/uncertain in this run: a dedicated Mars Odyssey THEMIS thermal inertia/global thermal products dataset did not surface via the catalog queries available here; if you specifically need THEMIS-derived thermal inertia or daytime/nighttime IR brightness temperature mosaics at orbital scale, that would require a targeted THEMIS-specific catalog/registry lookup beyond the matches returned in this run.

7. Decision Gate (what to do next)
